findings.description: Header seal/logo graphic and handwritten signature are missing from the main readable HTML content.
findings.evidence: In the PDF page 1, the North Carolina seal appears at the top center and a blue handwritten signature appears above the typed name block. In the HTML, the readable upright content shows only text; the seal and signature appear only inside an upside-down embedded image area, not integrated with the main reading view.

findings.description: Letterhead styling and typographic hierarchy are reduced in the HTML.
findings.evidence: In the PDF page 1, official names/titles in the left header are blue and italicized in parts, with centered agency branding. In the HTML, these appear as plain gray/black text with reduced emphasis and weaker visual distinction.

findings.description: HTML includes duplicated page content in the wrong visual sequence due to an upside-down inserted rendering above the readable text.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the HTML shows a full upside-down reproduction of the page near the top/middle, then repeats the same content again in readable upright text below. A sighted user scanning top-to-bottom encounters duplicated and misoriented content before the actual reading order.

findings.description: Overall page structure is not preserved because an upside-down duplicate rendering is inserted above the semantic content.
findings.evidence: The PDF page 1 is a single clean letter page. The HTML page 1 contains a large rotated duplicate image occupying substantial space, followed by the actual text content, altering whitespace groupings and page structure.

findings.description: Signatures are not preserved in the readable HTML content.
findings.evidence: In the PDF page 2, blue signatures are present for Brian Wrenn and Brenda Harris. In the HTML, the readable upright text lists the names but the signatures appear only in an upside-down embedded rendering, not in the main content flow.

findings.description: Heading emphasis and line styling are weakened in the HTML.
findings.evidence: The PDF page 2 has a centered bold uppercase title with underlining and strong separation from body text. The HTML uses enlarged underlined text but with different alignment and reduced fidelity to the original typographic presentation.

findings.description: Duplicated upside-down content disrupts reading order.
findings.evidence: On page 2, the HTML first presents an upside-down reproduction of the notice, then repeats the content in upright text below. This causes readers to encounter the same information twice and in the wrong visual order.

findings.description: Page layout is altered by insertion of a rotated duplicate block above the readable notice.
findings.evidence: The PDF page 2 is a single-page notice with centered content and signature blocks near the lower half. The HTML page 2 adds a large upside-down duplicate section before the readable content, changing overall structure and whitespace.
